title: Interview with Dr. Kiran Mazumdar Shaw, Founder & Executive-Chairperson of Biocon

# Interview with Dr. Kiran Mazumdar Shaw, Founder & Executive-Chairperson of Biocon

* [Report this article](https://www.linkedin.com/uas/login?session_redirect=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.linkedin.com%2Fpulse%2Finterview-dr-kiran-mazumdar-shaw-founder-biocon-janmejaya-sinha&trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_ellipsis-menu-semaphore-sign-in-redirect&guestReportContentType=PONCHO_ARTICLE&_f=guest-reporting)

[Janmejaya Sinha](https://in.linkedin.com/in/janmejaya-sinha-591259120)

### Janmejaya Sinha

Published Feb 18, 2021

[\+ Follow](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=%2Fpulse%2Finterview-dr-kiran-mazumdar-shaw-founder-biocon-janmejaya-sinha%3F_l%3Den&trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse_publisher-author-card)

> Tenacity, innovation, and the road less traveled!

**_Kiran Mazumdar Shaw_** _is the founder-chairperson of Biocon, India’s leading Biotech company._

_Both Dr. Shaw and Biocon have many firsts to their name. Biocon is the first pure-play biopharma company to be listed in India, the first Indian company to commercialize Biosimilars in American and Japanese markets, and the only Asian company to be consistently ranked among ‘top Biotech employers’ globally by Science Magazine. As of December 2019, Biocon was among 10 largest Biotech players by both revenue and market cap in Asia (ex-Australia). They have delivered a TSR of over 16% over the 10-year period into December 2019 (in USD terms) and are among the largest producers of statins and immunosuppressants globally. Dr. Shaw is seen as a role model for women entrepreneurs around the world. Forbes recognized Biocon among Asia’s 200 Best Under a Billion in 2019, and Dr. Shaw among the World’s Most Powerful Women in 2020._

_Biocon is an integrated Biotech company listed in India._

### How do you define success as a leader?

Success to me, is identifying an opportunity ahead of the curve, and then delivering on it in a way that keeps you in the lead. When you are bringing something new to the market, and especially if you are a new entrepreneur, success also depends on your ability to quickly build credibility and win the confidence of the market at large.

### What factors have differentiated your success as a leader?

Biosimilars was an opportunity I spotted much ahead of the market. At the time, it was seen as too gestational by investors. Building success in this business required building credibility, and particularly so, as a new, woman leader. There are many stereotypes and perception hurdles associated with women leadership that I have overturned along the way: I am ambitious, aggressive, competitive, I have the big picture, and I think global.

Being a global player comes with its own set of challenges: you need to navigate international regulations and build credibility for your product and its quality in global markets. A lot of organizations devalue themselves by limiting their vision to Emerging Markets. For me, it was always about the global opportunity. I was committed to addressing the challenges that it brings. Today, we are the only Indian company supplying Biosimilars to the most advanced markets of the world.

I have focused on understanding the competitive landscape of my market very well – and then building a niche, where we can be in a leadership position that is hard to overtake. We have built differentiation by focusing on high risk, high reward opportunities where relatively fewer people are willing to compete. Our valuation once we delivered on our premise has reflected this.

### Can you tell us a bit about your journey of becoming an entrepreneur?

I would describe myself as an ‘accidental entrepreneur’. I wanted to work as a ‘brew master’ but did not get that job! Around that time, an Irish entrepreneur who wanted to develop an enzymes business using India as R&D hub approached me. I decided to take that up. It was interesting times! It was a new space, Banks were reluctant to lend, prospective employees were hesitant about working for a woman entrepreneur – but I was young and committed my energy and time to building a strong base and generating confidence in the business. I am fortunate that my family has always been extremely supportive of my goals.

### What additional hurdles do entrepreneurs face as women?

We spoke about some of the stereotypes associated with women leaders that I had to overcome when I started out: women were not considered risk takers, not aggressive and competitive enough, lacking the big picture. These are preconceived biases that we had to challenge and shatter among investors and the eco-system.

At the same time, women often tend to be more diffident in their expression. They will underplay even a great idea they have. I have consciously always challenged these beliefs. I speak my mind, with fierce conviction, and along with compassion and empathy.

### How have success factors for leadership changed over time?

In the past, leadership was focused primarily on market share. At the same time, it was more difficult to succeed with ideas that were seen as ‘ahead’ of their time.

Today, ‘brand-value’ is critical. Regulatory challenges, on the other hand, are hindering growth and development in the pharma sector – for instance, in the e-pharmacy space.

In the future, tech-leadership will be the differentiator of success. Leaders will need to build leadership in their own technological niche. Companies that are not set up to be agile and adaptive to changes in the technological environment will get left behind.

### What differences do you perceive between leadership in Asia and the West? How do you see these evolving in the future?

Asian companies have focused more on building market dominance through scale. In the West, we see more organizations that have focused on innovation to drive value. As they do this, they have outsourced scale to grow globally – thus creating value from both drivers.

Value capture based only on scale is limited in the long run. This is why I have tried to follow the innovation model from the start. On scale, we have potential to continue to do more as an organization. To succeed in the future, companies in Asia will need to continuously innovate to maintain sustained leadership.

The local eco-system in our country can be challenging for innovation. I have taken a few assets and developed them overseas. Having a global mindset and developing your product in the right environment is important.

Management tends to be more centralized in Asia. As we grow, especially in a future that requires rapid response and adaptivity, decentralized models of control will become increasingly important.

If leveraged effectively, technology will allow us to bridge many of the differences or gaps we see between the East and the West over time.

### What advice would you give to a budding entrepreneur?

I have always challenged my people to think about the question: “how can we be different?”. Focusing on building differentiation thoughtfully is of utmost importance. It will pave the way for you to be both innovative and strategic, building a foundation for sustained success. Leaders and organizations that only ‘follow’, cannot be successful over the long term.

The one thing I would say is: “Don’t be a me-too entrepreneur! Think about how you can be differentiated, even if it is in a similar business line”.
